sentences.text: No, Rosa, yeah.
So I returned to the ghetto, very upset and very disappointed.
It was also not easy to smuggle yourself in, and to take this-- if you would be found out that you went out, and went in, it was a terrible danger.
But you know, in this circumstances, you have to choose between danger and trying to do something for yourself.
Anyway, I related it to Rosa.
And she was very upset.
And I was very upset.
And I said, look, what happens to you will happen to me.
I'll stay here.
And things went worse-- from worse to worse, from worse.
And all the time rumors, and-- and the building of the bunkers, and you-- but we had a little food more, because we were in the-- working in the kitchens.
And then after-- it must have been a month, or even more, by chance, again, you know, sometimes faith is, you know-- it's very, very precarious.
And I met my friend, Michael.
Because he-- from time to time, he inquired what happens.
And he was--
The same friend.
Yes, Michael--
--the same friend, who helped me--
--who persuaded me.
And he was horrified that I came back.
And I said, I came back, Michael, because Rosa is here.
And I don't feel like leaving Rosa.
And I-- and I don't know what-- I can't-- I don't know how to approach the-- to take her out, without the means, without the shelter, without having the first step where to go.
And my cousin absolutely refuses.
Because he has already his mother on his shoulders.
He said, still you should go.
Because somebody should be alive to tell the story, what happened to all of us.
And you should go.
And you should try to-- you have the shelter.
And, anyway, then my step--
That's Michael, this friend of mine.
Yes.
But then-- and Rosa gradually said, look, you should go.
Because maybe you could make your way to this little villa we had.
Maybe somebody will be saved.
Maybe somebody you can help.
You are young.
Go.
And you know, this was-- it was a terrible decision.
And that is, you know, something which one never forgets, this very, very traumatic experience, when you have to make a choice between life and death, you know.
Terrible.
And I was young.
I wanted to live.
I thought, I'll go.
But it was, you know, very tragic that I couldn't save Rosa-- never mind the others.
I tried my best for everybody, for all the-- for my father, for Michael, for Rudolph.
I helped.
I did-- but not for Rosa.
And she was anyway downhearted already with the-- with the loss of her husband, the loss of the son--
[? --and ?] [? things ?] [? like ?] that.
Anyway, one day, I-- and she was with-- she moved to her cousin.
Because you could move to the premises.
She had a cousin, what I mentioned before, was a very nice lady.
And she said, look, I'll be with her.
Whatever happens, we'll be together.
In the ghetto.
And I went to-- left the ghetto for-- left-- left the ghetto for good.
Went to my cousin--
Yes, yes, yes.
No, no, no.
This-- yes, this was a very tragic, very terrible thing.
Because this Michael tried again and again to smuggle Jews.
And he did smuggle a lot.
And then, when I was already on the Christian side, I one day telephoned from a safe telephone.
And the father answered--
To Michael's father.
I didn't see Michael afterwards anymore.
Because I left the ghetto.
And I said to Michael-- to-- to--
--and I said, who's [INAUDIBLE] Michael?
Don't you know?
And I said, don't I know what?
He was shot dead.
On one of the last, almost that he decided he will not do it anymore-- they persuaded him, maybe you shouldn't go anymore, because you are being recognized.
He approached the-- he took a batch of people, number of people.
And he thought that he can bribe, as usual.
And that was probably set up for him.
And that fellow at the gate, the soldiers-- soldier at the gate-- either he knew him, or he didn't want to know-- shot him dead.
Just like that.
That was a terrible blow altogether.
But he saved a lot of people, including me.
I would never have gone out of the ghetto-- never, never, never, never.
But he was so persistent, you know.
And-- so then I settled with my cousin.
They took my paper-- took the paper-- I don't know why I took the papers with me.
And they put it in the oven-- you know, the Russian type of [INAUDIBLE] ovens.
Ghetto paper-- of my birth certificate-- some papers I took with-- some few photographs, which survived, and so-- and few bits of clothing.
But the papers they put in there-- it was not proper winter.
So they put it in the oven.
Oh, it was-- yes, it must have been now already the beginning of '43.
Yes, it was the beginning of '43, 1943.
And we were [INAUDIBLE].
They were very nice to me.
And they had the whole family-- very anti-Semitic family.
He was a Jew, born-- married to this family, the Polish family.
They were very anti-Semitic.
But in spite of being very anti-Semitic, they kept my aunt with one of their relatives, in a place called Zielonka, which was a holiday place near Warsaw, 20 minutes away by train.
Anyway, I was with them.
And after a few months, they decided that we'll go to the Zielonka.
And they will hire a very nice-- rent a very nice flat.
And we'll be there, like on a holiday.
And my cousins will commute for work.
He was a very clever man, my cousin-- extremely clever.
He spoke German fluently.
And he worked for a German firm, which was-- which dealt with iron scrap.
And you would know-- very well known, the Germans were very keen on every scrap of metal for re-melting, and doing tanks, or whatever.
And he worked for one of them, a very big firm, and earned a very good living.
And they couldn't imagine he was a Jew.
He didn't look-- part of our family didn't look Jewish at all.
And he was one of them.
My father didn't look, either-- blue eyes and blond hair.
And one day he invited, to this holiday place, where I was the nanny to the little girl, and went to the shop, to the villages-- nobody imagined that I was Jewish.
But I often heard remarks, like, you know, when the ghetto was burning, in 1943, in April, that-- that a very good think that the Jews are being fried and finished.
And I said to myself, if I ever-- I never believed it.

But if I'd ever survive, I'll never return to this damned place-- a damned place, never [? is there. ?] And I-- you can imagine in my heart how I was listening to this story that the-- and the-- and the burning wind was bringing even smells sometimes from the-- from the ghetto.
The ghetto was burning forever.
Because it was like 1920-- in Ap-- April, 1943, was the Jewish upraising.
It was a very heroic thing-- a few people there.
They did a lot of damage to the tanks, to the German tanks, and to the Germans.
But eventually, they had like-- like in Masada, they had-- they committed suicide, by [? running ?] down.
Anyway, this cousin of mine invited this German director.
I was shivering, absolutely, from fright.
But he was not afraid.
He invited him for a weekend.
He invited him for the weekend.
And then they-- it didn't occur to the German that I could be Jewish, looking after the little girl.
And then, in the-- this-- during the day, they went to a place for either fishing, or the boat.
They brought in a big bucket of little crabs.
And they brought it home.
And my cousin was supposed to cook it the next day.
In Poland, you used to eat a lot of these crabs.
I was sleeping in the kitchen.
That was my place.
And in the night, I got up, for one reason or the other.
And suddenly, I saw that the whole floor was walking!
The whole floor was walking.
It was unbelievable.
It was completely-- I have realized that they didn't cover these crabs.
And they made their way all over.
It was a frightening experience-- funny and awful.
I had to pick each crab, and put in the bucket, and cover it.
Next day, it was all plunged into boiling water.
And they made a big feast and ate, and ate, and cooked, and drank-- drank vodka.
My cousin was excellent at it.
Anyway, soon after this-- yes, very soon after that-- he brought his mother from the place in Zielonka, where she was hiding, into the apartment where we lived.
And it was quite dangerous.
Because the concierge were downstairs.
The concierge, at any rate, you know, are very nosy people.
But these particular Polish ones-- [INAUDIBLE].
And they decided-- and they-- and they-- sort of, they made illicit-- illicit drinks.
Anyway, she was not allowed to walk, talk-- she was allowed to walk, but not to talk.
So like, she would be an invisible living person.
And she lived with us.
And after a few months, living like that-- very short time, one or two months-- one night, my cousin, the little girl, and-- the three of them disappeared.
We got up in the morning with my aunt.
And they were not there.
Either they were frightened, or afraid, or didn't want to-- afterwards, we asked them.
And I met them after the war.
They left us helpless like that-- me and my old aunt.
Without even-- that was in the Zielonka.
But I might mention that during the course of the few months, we met a family called [?
Kerschnerski. ?] We met the [?
Kerschnerski ?] family, and they were very charming people--
In Zielonka-- who were some-- who were friends of the woman who was hiding my aunt.
And this family, [?
Kerschnerski, ?] were not quite stupid.
They realized that something is fishy here with me, and this, and that, and that.
Anyway--
No, no, no.
They Polish, a Polish people.
And then I had to make my way to the village, and to do some stitching, because we didn't have any money to live.
They didn't leave us any-- it was a very-- a very, very cruel thing to do, leave like that.
But I supposed it was my cousin-- his wife.
Because she-- she couldn't stand my mother-in-law.
And she said, why should I die-- die for these Jews?
And if-- if they went during the day, we would be-- you know, it would be a scene.
So they went during the night.
And I had to go to the village-- invented the stories that my cousin left-- this, and this, and the other.
And we are waiting-- I am waiting for him.
Not we-- I am waiting.
And I had to go to the village in the night, in the evening-- come back in the evening.
Made with my sign that if everything is all right, she should put-- she should put the light in the--
--in the window.
Then I know that nobody-- one day, downstairs, this concierge already had an idea that something is here going on.
And he-- I got very frightened of him-- very frightened.
Because I was afraid that next day, he could call the-- the--
The-- the-- the police.
He could-- he only didn't call the police because he was-- he was doing this-- this illicit drink.
And he was forcing me sometimes that I should drink it.
I was very scared that what will happen.
And I said-- one evening, I came to my auntie.
And I said, we must try enlist the help of the [?
Kerschnerskis ?] family.
And we decided that we'll do this, this, and this [INAUDIBLE].
We put clothes-- whatever we had-- one, on top of the other one, on top of the other.
We waited till it was completely quiet, maybe three o'clock in the night.
And before we just go, and I got a sort of nervous breakdown.
And I started shaking.
I shook so violently that I couldn't talk.
I couldn't move.
I couldn't do anything.
Yeah.
It just-- too much was happening to me.
And I-- we had to overcome this.
She talked to me.
She was a very religious person, my auntie.
Eventually, it subsided a little bit.
And we went down very quietly, leaving everything-- only the clothes we had, and the small two bundles-- and made our way, three o'clock in the night, to this [?
Kerschnerski ?] family.
And can you imagine in the night-- the wind blowing in the darkness, the fright, the fear of the unknown.
And-- and we down, made our way, eventually stopped at the door.
We knew, because we met these people.
And opposite him-- I have all these photographs made in 1995.
And opposite him was the house where my auntie was previously hiding.
And he opened the door, you know, in the middle of the night, frightened, thinking maybe Germans.
Then he sees two women, two Jewish-- he knew, straight away.
One old Jewish woman looked like very Jewish woman, and me-- poor, huddled together, frightened to death.
And standing there, he had the presence of mind to let us in.
And then I explained to him the story what has happened.
No, no, no.
I was not afraid.
There was no way.
Where would I go?
I had no way.
No, there was not a soul I knew.
That's why-- these people-- for 50 years, I have been supporting with money, with clothes, with everything, with help from highers from America.
And they got, eventually, recognized by the--
--Righteous, as Righteous people, and inscribed in books, and certificates, and all that.
And then he said, look.
I will-- I will-- you don't need to hide, he said to me.
You will live with us.
You don't need to hide.
And aunt will go straight away to my aunt.
Because she can't be with us.
She's-- she looks too Jewish.
And she has to be hiding completely.
So he took her in the night, straight away.
The-- and the woman kept her.
It was-- it was a good luck that she retained her.
You see, there were some good people among Jew-- Polish people.
Even those-- those few Pole-- Jewish people [INAUDIBLE]-- they were saved by them.
And I lived with them, almost eight months.
In this village.
But--
What did I do?
Well I did-- I did-- to earn my living-- and they were-- they were so poor-- that was just not true.
He was a railway man.
He earned very little money on the railway.
She was at home, with a little boy of four.
And so I-- I always liked stitching.
And you know, in-- in a situation-- in a situation, you know, you invent.
I-- I learned some stitching at school.
I knew how to make patterns, you know.
And I employed all kinds of little tricks to learn more.
And I-- from the village, from people I knew already, I earned quite a good living.
But I used to work till late at night.
You know, there was no electricity.
We didn't have electricity in that village.
So there was a [? carbide ?] lamp, which was, you know-- it had a-- a certain-- that was on-- a certain-- I can't describe to you.
But it was an artific-- made an artificial light-- very strong.
It was very, very bad for the eyes.
And my eyes are not good at all.
And I used to do embroidery and all kinds of things.
Met a charming family called, [?
Kosheva. ?] They was-- he was a professor of the-- he invented the first Polish alphabet.
And they befriended-- I befriended them.
They befriended me.
And the daughter was getting married to a sort of nobleman, and so on.
They asked me to move to their place.
And I made the whole trousseau.
I never-- I made fur coats, and a wedding dress, and everything possible you can imagine I made for them.
And earned-- whatever I earned went straight to [?
Kerschnerski's ?] family-- coal, and food.
And so I really supported them during this time.
I was staying-- I was staying with them for about-- for about [INAUDIBLE].
But Jew-- it was at a certain time, one night-- it was early morning, maybe five o'clock in the morning.
The German police, you know, with that car, with the noise, with the uniform, with the-- with the rifles, with the helmets, arrived in the middle of the night.
What happened was, at some point, two couples arrived in the Zielonka.
One lived next door to our-- it was like a little-- lived like, in in a wooden-- wooden villa, and opposite, another couple.
They were very friend-- good friends.
I was in the attic there.
And I looked out.
And the Germans took the couple from that, straight away.
And as they were taking them out, [?
Roman, ?] who was a [?
Kerschnerski, ?] Roman [?
Kerschnerski, ?] run up, and said, Hanu he said-- Hanu was an abbreviation of my name-- run!
He had my coat.
I was naked, except for the-- for the nightie and-- and shoes.
Threw the coat on top of me.
He said, run!
The room was so situated that a window was pro-- was-- was opening into a-- into the field.
And the field led to a sort of small-- led to the forest, and then a cemetery.

And I just, in this, you know, state of panic, ran again into this-- towards this [? thing. ?] As I was running towards the cemetery, the couple next door, who [? subletted-- ?] it was just Christmas, two days to Christmas-- dressed with boots, and shoes, and coats, and fur coats, and whatnot.
And they ran with me.
So I realized that they were Jews, as well.
Didn't know before-- a little bit he looked to me like an-- but you know, they-- they looked-- they looked so well.
And they was-- they had plenty of money, unlike me.
I was-- what I earned, everything was going away.
And we run into the cemetery.
And there, at the cemetery, we heard the shots.
They were-- they were killed outright, these two people.
They were relations, or they were cousins-- I didn't enquiry.
We were absolutely shocked.
And they-- I was naked, practically.
So-- but they had double-- double socks, and double panties, and double scarves.
And-- and so they dressed me up, as best as they could.
And together, we went to Warsaw.
They had a room there.
And there we had a sort of conference, what to do.
It was-- the same night was the-- was the Christmas Eve.
And they said-- and everything was prepared in Roman's place.
It was going to be a big feast, and so on-- saving food and fat, for months on end.
And so we said, if we don't return to that place, the people-- they say that we are Jews, and in the, you know, [INAUDIBLE] of Jewish people, some of them were, the [? religious ?] or so on.
They will-- they will be marked.
So the best thing is to go back.
So they-- it was a sort of bravura, sort of brav-- because, you know, we knew it is a dangerous thing to come back.
But yet, we decided to go, to risk it.
I was already not feeling very well.
But they let me sleep for few hours.
I must have had already fever.
Where?
Where?
In room they had.
In Warsaw.
They went shopping.
They brought a lot of goods.
And they were-- they must-- they must have been stuffed with money.
And they were-- took a lot of provisions, and things.
And we went back.
And we were very, very, very nicely received.
Because it means for the-- for the Roman, and the villagers, that--
--we were there.
And we came back, went shopping, and we didn't run away.
You understand.
Then we decided that the feast will take place-- what do you know they have-- they had a family coming from Warsaw.
But it was decided there would be a vigilance, that now and again, one of the men would stay outside.
And they will listen, in case somebody comes.
And we-- we had a little vodka, which was good, because you know, that I was--
--so tired and all of this-- exhausted.
And at certain point, we hear somebody says, cars are coming.
Light and cars are coming.
So we're sure the Germans are coming.
Again-- run.
So-- so I run.
And in this running-- forward in the-- it was a different, into the forest.
There were also ditches there.
And I fell into a ditch.
And I imagine, in my feverish imagination-- because the wind was blowing, and rustling the-- the the-- not the trees, but the twigs.
And so, I was imagining that the dogs are after me.
I must have lost the concious.
After I regain conscious, I was in bed.
Now, what happened?
The cars was an innocent car.
They went for the-- for the feast, to friends.
Because it was near Warsaw.
It was a fashionable place.
They lost the way.
They came to find the way.
So afterwards, they searched for me.
They couldn't find me for a long time.
Because becasue-- snow was covered-- freezing.
Christmas.
And I had pneumonia.
So there was no way they could call a doctor.
Because they were scared.
So they fed me.
They gave me vodka, and hot tea, and hot tea, and vodka.
And I survived.
And after that, you know, I didn't tell the family, for whom I was working, what has happened.
But I returned.
And they said, what happened to you?
You look so terrible.
Oh, I said, I had an appendix attack, or one thing, or the other.
But, I must tell you, a curious thing happened.
Because-- but at that time, they told me, it was already at the end-- end of '43.
I knew that as the place was burned out, I couldn't go back to Zielonka.
I said practically goodbye to my friends.
And this Roman worked for the German-- German garage.
And he was [? spraying ?] cars-- in the night, mostly.
He also got tuberculosis from that.
And after the war, I sent him streptomycin, and Penicillin, and he was saved.
Yes.
And the only thing was that I should-- during the day, they gave me a temporary-- this-- the family, this professor, and [?
Kosheva-- ?] I didn't divulge who I was.
But they had a pretty good idea already.
And they gave me an address of a lady that I could be there for a short time.
In the night-- so I could be there for a short time.
So I went to Warsaw and met this lady.
She was not too keen.
But she allowed me two weeks to stay.
And I said, goodbye to Roman, and to this [?
Kosheva ?] family, and after that, I had nowhere to stay.
I had absolutely no-- Roman allowed me to come to the garage, which was outskirts of Warsaw--
Because it was a danger.
I-- oh, yes, yes, yes.
Oh, I forgot.
You are quite right.
One day-- one day, while I was still--
And it seemed to be that things came to normal and--
We stayed there.
Suddenly, another batch of Germans came to Zielonka, searching for Jews.
They were told there are Jews in that village.
Why it happened?
Because one of the lady-- one or two-- were buying a lot of food.
And she was a widow, all alone in a house.
And so they were under suspicion.
And you know, this-- the intelligentsia didn't help, but didn't condemn, and did not report.
But the middle-- lower-middle class was very keen, all the time, to report, and denounce-- all the time.
So they were very keen to denounce.
And they perceived that somebody is hiding Jews in the village, in that Zielonka.
And they wanted to find out.
They told the Germans.
